coredata.dc:title: Surface imprinted polymer on dual emitting MOF functionalized with blue copper nanoclusters and yellow carbon dots as a highly specific ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Pirot, Shano M.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: The rational incorporation of metal organic frameworks (MOFs) into molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) shows excellent potential for improving various merits from both approaches and elimination of the drawbacks of the counterparts. In this work, surface imprinted polymer on zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) are presented as a highly selective probe for detection of ascorbic acid (AA). An MIP layer was surface imprinted on nano-sized ZIF-8 with AA as a template, and blue copper nanocluster (B-CuNCs) and yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) as fluorescent signal transducers. The MIP capped ZIF-8 (MIP@ZIF-8) offered selective cavities for AA detection. The integrated system of MIP@ZIF-8 consisted of two emitters, CuNCs (λem 420 nm) used as a reference signal inserted in the pores of the ZIF-8, and yellow carbon dot (CDs, λem 560 nm) used as a probe and inserted in the polymer layer. The yellow emission of MIP@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was selectively quenched after addition of ascorbic acid, while the blue emission remained unaffected and used as an internal reference signal. The MIP@ZIF-8 ratiometry probe showed a dynamic linear response to AA in the range 4.0 µM – 22.0 µM with a detection limit 1.56 µM. The ratiometry-based fluorescence probe was successfully used for detection AA in drug samples and human serum with satisfactory recoveries of 92.5–106.11 % and 81.69 – 93.89 % respectively. Additional to high selectivity and sensitivity obtained from this MIP-MOF design, the presented probe, showed highly stability in room temperatures under well-lit room, which qualifies the probe for long-term use.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Metal organic frame work
Ratiometric fluorescence
ZIF-8
Copper nanoclusters

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A: Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) B-CuNCs and (b) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8. B: Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 -inset (a), (b) Y-CDs-inset (b) and (c) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs- inset (c) C: FT-IR spectrum of (a) ZIF-8 (black line), (b) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 (red line), (c) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs (blue line), and (d) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs (green line). D: XRD patterns of ZIF-8, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, B-CuNCs@ZIF8/Y-CDs@MIP and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIP.
TEM image of; A: is CuNCs@ZIF-8. B: Fe-SEM image of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8. C: TEM image of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP. D: FE-SEM image of B-CuNCs@ZIF8/Y-CDs@MIP.

(A, B) Selectivity test of Y-CDs, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/CDs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs for different metal ions and biological molecules. (C) Fluorescence spectra of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs probe in various pHs. (D) The stability fluorescence intensity of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP.
A: the fluorescence spectra of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP. B: calibration curve. C: fluorescence spectra of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIP. D: calibration curve of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIP.
A Stern–Volmer plot, B the absorbance spectra of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs without AA and with AA.
Results of the spike recoveries for the detection AA in Vitamin C tablets and serum fluid (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * Serum 1 was a female, 25 years old, not taking any Vitamin C tablet.
Serum 2 was a female, 25 years old, not taking any Vitamin C tablet.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of our present method with other methods used for detection AA.

head.ce:title: Surface imprinted polymer on dual emitting MOF functionalized with blue copper nanoclusters and yellow carbon dots as a highly specific ratiometric fluorescence probe for ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biomedical Analysis, Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St,46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biomedical Analysis, Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St,46002, Sulaimani City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational incorporation of metal organic frameworks (MOFs) into molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) shows excellent potential for improving various merits from both approaches and elimination of the drawbacks of the counterparts. In this work, surface imprinted polymer on zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) are presented as a highly selective probe for detection of ascorbic acid (AA). An MIP layer was surface imprinted on nano-sized ZIF-8 with AA as a template, and blue copper nanocluster (B-CuNCs) and yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) as fluorescent signal transducers. The MIP capped ZIF-8 (MIP@ZIF-8) offered selective cavities for AA detection. The integrated system of MIP@ZIF-8 consisted of two emitters, CuNCs (λem 420 nm) used as a reference signal inserted in the pores of the ZIF-8, and yellow carbon dot (CDs, λem 560 nm) used as a probe and inserted in the polymer layer. The yellow emission of MIP@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was selectively quenched after addition of ascorbic acid, while the blue emission remained unaffected and used as an internal reference signal. The MIP@ZIF-8 ratiometry probe showed a dynamic linear response to AA in the range 4.0 µM – 22.0 µM with a detection limit 1.56 µM. The ratiometry-based fluorescence probe was successfully used for detection AA in drug samples and human serum with satisfactory recoveries of 92.5–106.11 % and 81.69 – 93.89 % respectively. Additional to high selectivity and sensitivity obtained from this MIP-MOF design, the presented probe, showed highly stability in room temperatures under well-lit room, which qualifies the probe for long-term use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), usually known as “vitamin C,” is a water-soluble vitamin found in fresh fruits, vegetables, and juices. AA is necessary for regulating the physiological function of the human body as a source of antioxidant, coenzyme factor, free radical scavenger, and antiviral agent. [1–3] Additionally, AA can be utilized to both prevent and treat illness. Low AA consumption causes a disruption in medication metabolism, a weakening of the immune system, and even scurvy symptoms. [4,5].

Several analytical methods have been developed for the determination of AA in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), [6,7] spectrophotometry, [8,9] gas chromatography, [10] and capillary electrophoresis. [11,12] However, the aforementioned techniques have some disadvantages, such as complicated sample preparation, expensive instrumentation, personnel skills, and inefficiency that hinder the applications of these techniques. In this regard, fluorescence-based methods with their excellent advantages, have appealed more attentions, as they are rapid and relatively simple in operations with great sensitivity. One of the drawbacks of fluorometric analysis is presence of many interfering species. Thus, introduction of a new platform for selectivity improvement is necessary for practical application purposes. In this context, one should search for a sensitive, selective, low-cost, easy to use, and reliable method.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthetic chemical receptors that bind to target molecules with high affinity.[13,14] Due to such a highly affinity in molecular recognition, it is called artificial antibody or mimetic antibody and it has been extensively used in biochemical sensing[15–18] Moreover, long-term chemical and physical stability, good reusability, and low-cost synthesis procedure, made MIPs best alternative for immuonoassays [19,20].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous hybrid materials prepared by the assembly between metal ions and organic linkers in appropriate solvents. [21] MOFs have many properties including variable pore sizes, high thermal stability, large surface area, structural diversity and tunable shapes. [22,23] Thus, MOFs are employed in various applications such as catalysis, [24] sensing, [25–28] adsorption, [29] drug transportation, [30] and energy. [31].

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) such as ZIF-8, as one of the most common and interesting representative MOFs, have shown many outstanding advantages such as easy synthesis, high porosity, aqueous dispensability, large surface area, and excellent ability for host matrix.[32] Recently, many reports published on functionalizing MOFs with luminescent materials, such as metal nanoclusters, [33,34] carbon nanomaterials, [35,36] and quantum dots.[37,38] The functionalized motifs of MOFs showed great appealing in biochemical sensing.[39,40] For instance,[41] Xuelian et al, for the first time determination of trace alpha-cypermethrin (α-CPM) based on a sensitive molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanosensor based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs).

Combination of MIPs and functionalized MOFs can synergize the merits of both, such as high affinity molecular recognition from MIP, large surface area and stability from MOFs.[42,43] The surface modification of functionalized MOFs by MIPs via surface imprinting can be very effective to achieve an integrated probe containing target-responsive MOFs with analyte selective MIP cavities. For instance, Yao et al,[44] developed a rapid method for determination tyramine in food based on a new molecular imprinted polymers combined metal‐organic frameworks UiO‐66 (named UiO‐66@MIPs) as the sensing recognition element for tyramine.

In the a few last years, ratiometry-based fluorescence sensor have attracted great attention. Ratiometric fluorescent sensors using two individual fluorescence emission wavelengths are developed to achieve self-calibration, which is because one emission peak maintains constant as internal reference and the other is sensitive to the analyte as signal report unit. Simultaneously, the instrumental error can be effectively eliminated, and the sensitivity of the sensor can be enhanced. [45,46].

As an optical transduction, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, such as carbon dots, received more attention for construction of fluorescent sensors because of their unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility, and convenient surface modification flexibility. [47–53] Another new family is metal nanoclusters, such as AuNCs, AgNCs and CuNCs which are widely used in various applications due to their excellent features including high fluorescence, excellent resistance to photobleaching, and biocompatibility compared with organic dyes and toxic quantum dots, low toxicity and unique size-dependent fluorescence properties. [54] CuNCs is the most studied and popular metal nanoclusters because of their good optical properties, abundant and readily available element compared to gold and silver. [55].

Inspired by the merits in MIP and MOF combinations, enhancing of the fluorescence emission via encapsulation in MOFs, and advantageous of ratiometry, herein, an integrated probe from MIP capped MOF was prepared incorporating dual fluorescence emitters. ZIF-8 functionalized with blue CuNCs (B-CuNCs) and yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) followed by surface imprinting with AA as a template. Emission intensity of B-CuNCs was enhanced inside the pores of ZIF-8, and the cavities imprinted in the capped layer in MIP were selective to AA. Y-CDs were quenched by AA, while emission of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 was remained unaffected. Such an integrated MIP-MOF ratiometry design were used to detect AA in serum fluid and pharmaceutical formulations. Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram for preparation of the emitters and MIP@ZIF-8 probe.

All the reagents used were of analytical grade purchased from commercial suppliers (Merck and Sigma-Aldrich) and were used without further purification. Thiamine hydrochloride, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O, % 98.5), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), 2-methylimidazole (HMIM), tetaethylorthosilicate (TEOS % 99.0,), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, % 98.0), ammonium solution (25 % NH4·H2O), ascorbic acid, O-phenylenediamine (O-PD), and methanol. Ultra-pure water was produced in our lab, and all metal salts were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

A Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to obtain the UV–VIS absorption spectra. Fluorescence spectra were recorded via Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), both the emission and excitations slits were set at 5.0 nm. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Varian 640 FTIR spectrophotometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA). Empyrean X-ray diffractometer, (PANalytical, Netherland) was used to collect the X-ray diffraction patterns XRD. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Zeiss-EM10C (100 KV, German) was used to characterize the morphology and particle size. A FE-SEM (field emission- scanning electron microscopy), EDS and elemental mapping was characterized by (Zeiss sigma 300).

B-CuNCs were papered based on reports published in literature with modifications. [56–58] In a typical experiment, 25 mg of thiamine hydrochloride was dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure deionized water, then 4 mL of CuSO4·5H2O (20 µM) added to the above solution. After the solution was stirred at room temperature for 5 min, the acidity of the solution pH was adjusted to 12 using 1 M NaOH until the color of solution changes from white hydrogel to purple. The mixture was reduced by quickly adding 3 mL of 1 M NaBH4, then the solution was stirred for 1.0 h at 55 °C. Finally, a brown solution was produced and shows blue emission under UV irradiation.

B-CuNCs@ZiF-8 was prepared according to the previous route with some modifications.[56] Briefly, ligand solution was prepared by dissolving 0.6 gm of 2-methylimidazole in 15 mL methanol (0.5 mol/L). Later, 2.5 mL CuNCs solution was added to the ligand solution. Metal ion solution was prepared by dissolving 0.2 gm zinc nitrate to 15 mL methanol (0.06 mol/L). Later, both the metal ions and the ligand solution were mixed together and stirred for 6 hrs. After collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm) and washed five times with deionized water and methanol, the product of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 was placed in a furnace at 65 °C.

Synthesis a B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs according to the previous work with some modifications. [59,60] 10 mL of Y-CDs added to 10 mg of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 was dispersed in 10 mL methanol and stirred for 1.0 h. Then, 50 µL APTES as functional monomer and 10 mg the template of AA was added to the above mixture with stirring for 2.0 h at room temperature. Subsequently, 200 µL cross-linker (TEOS) and 200 µL NH3 were added. After the mixture was magnetic stirred at room temperature for 12 h, Finally, the solid product was centrifuged, washed and dried. The templates of AA were eluted by Soxhlet extractor using a mixture of water and methanol (1:2, v/v). In comparison, the nonimprinted polymers (B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs) were also constructed by the same method but without addition of AA.

In a typical assay, 1.0 mL of the probe B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs suspension in 5.0 mL volumetric flask. Then different concentrations of AA (0 µM to 30 µM) solution were prepared by adding different volume of AA stock solution. The final solution was completed to the mark with deionized water. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 400–650 nm with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm.

A drug samples of ascorbic acid capsule was achieved from drug store. For representative sampling, 10 tablets of Vitamin C drug were weighed out and crashed in a motor. From this crushed powder, 0.2 gm was dissolved in water in 250 mL deionized water. A 1.0 mL of each real drug sample was spiked by adding different amount of standard ascorbic acid solutions (4.0 µM, 8.0 µM, and 12.0 µM). The mixture was then diluted by water, after addition of 1.0 mL of our probe B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs. Body fluid serum samples were obtained from volunteers’ normal (healthy) persons. This was approved by Ethic Committee of the graduate unit of the College of Science in the university of Sulaimani. A 50 µL of each serum sample was spiked by adding different concentrations of standard ascorbic acid solutions, 4.0, 8.0, and 12.0 µM, and the mixture was diluted by water after addition a 1.0 mL of our probe B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs.

As shown in Fig. 1, nanocomposite of the blue emission B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 was prepared by encapsulation of B-CuNCs in ZIF-8. Then the polymer was surface imprinted through sol-gel method on the surface of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 that was coated with CDs. Here the condensation reaction between APTES and TEOS was catalyzed by aqueous ammonia solution in the presence of ascorbic acid as a template. The strong interaction between amino group of APTES and hydroxyl portion of ascorbic acid supported the specific recognition of the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs toward ascorbic acid. [61] After ascorbic acid was removed by Soxhlet extraction (methanol:water, 2:1 v/v), the empty imprinted cavities were obtained in the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of B-CuNCs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 composite are shown in Fig. 2 A. The blue emission of B-CuNCs showed a maximum fluorescence emission at 420 nm under excitation at 375 nm. However, the pure ZIF-8 has no obvious emission when excited at 375 nm. When B-CuNCs encapsulated in ZIF-8, the fluorescence intensity increases dramatically. The relative photoluminescence QYs of B-CuNCs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 were determined to be 12 % and 53 %, respectively. Such enhancement is highly significant in chemical analysis.

Fig. 2B displays the fluorescence spectra of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, Y-CDs, and the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs. The fluorescence spectra of the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite had an emission peak at 420 nm after excitation at 375 nm (Fig. 2B, curve a), and it showed strong deep blue luminescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (inset a). However, Y-CDs' yellow fluorescence (Fig. 2B, inset b) displayed a maximum fluorescence at 560 nm (Fig. 2B, curve b). Under 375 nm excitation, two highly defined emission peaks at 420 nm and 560 nm (Fig. 2B, curve c) were obtained when the two fluorescence emitters, Y-CDs and B-CuNCs, were introduced into the MIP. Under UV irradiation at 365 nm, the emission of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs showed light yellow fluorescence (Fig. 2B, inset c). The UV–Vis spectra of B-CuNCs, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, Y-CDs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs were recorded and shown in Fig. S1(S1, Supplementary information).

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, B-CuNC@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs, and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs. The FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 peaks at 2920 –3165 cm−1 reveal the stretching of the aliphatic and aromatic CH of the imidazole ring, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2C. The imidazole ring bends in-plane and out-of-plane at 690–755 and 950–1173 cm−1, respectively. The stretching of the Zn–N bond is shown in the peak at 422 cm−1. The absorption band of the CN bond may be seen in sharp peaks about 1302–1420 cm−1. [62] The B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 FT-IR spectra showed a wide peak at 3130–2650 cm−1 and a vanishing peak Zn-N at 422 cm−1. The result showed that B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 had formed. [58,63,64] Furthermore, there was a strong band similarity between B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs. The wide and strong absorption peak at 1044 cm−1 was a characteristic signal of typical asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching, suggesting the existence of SiO2 matrices, and the characteristic peaks at 462 cm−1 and 784 cm−1 may be attributed to stretching vibrations of Si-O. Furthermore, the presence of the aminopropyl group of APTES was shown by the stretching vibration bands of NH around 3420 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1 and the aliphatic CH stretching band at 2920 cm−1, indicating that the sol-gel polymerization procedure had been effectively accomplished. The data showed that the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs were effectively constructed. [59].

XRD spectra were used for analysis the crystalline structure of ZIF-8, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs, and the results were displayed in Fig. 2D. The results showed that both ZIF-8 and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 have mostly similar crystalline structure and sharp diffraction peak. The encapsulation B-CuNCs into ZIF-8 did not destroy the structure of ZIF-8. Furthermore, the intensity of diffraction peak of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs was significantly decreased compared with the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8. Nevertheless, the spectra displayed one broad characteristic diffraction peak (20–25°) of amorphous SiO2, as supported in literature.[59,65].

The morphology and size of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 and B-CuNCs@ZIF8/CDs@MIP was characterized using FE-SEM and TEM, as shown in Fig. 3 A. The shape of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 was nearly spherical with an average size of 30 nm (Fig. 3A), and it was in an agreement with FE-SEM in Fig. 3B. The surface imprinted growth of MIP on the surface B-CuNCs@ZIF-8Y-/CDs was observed in Fig. 3C, as the average size is between 30 and 35 nm. The corresponding FE-SEM image of imprinted polymer of B-CuNCs@ZIF8/Y-CDs@MIP is shown in Fig. 3D. Moreover, elemental mapping using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. S2E-K, Supplementary information) revealed that all atoms, C, O, N, Zn and Si were all existed and uniformly distributed across the material area, which further demonstrated the CuNCs@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs was successfully fabricated. The EDS spectra for B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP shown in Fig. S3 (Supplementary information). More images TEM and FE-SEM of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP see in Figs. S4 and S5 (Supplementary information).

The fluorescence emission of Y-CDs was quenched upon the addition of AA, while B-CuNCs remained unaffected. This can be exploited to design a ratiometry-based probe which blue emission is as a reference signal and yellow emission is as a probe. Both emitters were inserted into the MIP and ZIF-8, MIP as a molecular recognition element for AA and ZIF-8 as the fluorescence emission enhancer for the emission of B-CuNCs.

To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor, the fluorescence response of Y-CDs, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/CDs and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs toward AA and other ions and molecules was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence quenching response of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs by AA was much higher than metal ions such as Cu2+, Co2+, Na2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Cd2+. Also as shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence quenching response of the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs by AA was higher than other biological molecules, like urea, uric acid, creatinine, glucose, lactose and sucrose. While most metals ions and biomolecules quenched the fluorescence emission of Y-CDs, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 and B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs as shown in Fig. 4A and 4B compered the probe B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/CDs@MIP. This means our integrated probe, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP, showed high selectivity towards AA due to the imprinted cavities on the surface of the ZIF-8.

In order to optimize sensitivity of the probe, pH of the working solution was optimized. The fluorescence intensity of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs at different pH values in the range of 1.0–12.0 were studied. As displayed in Fig. 4C, emission of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 (λemission 420 nm) and Y-CDs (λemission 560 nm) reached maximum value when pH was from 6.0−10.0. In the low pH, pHs 1.0 to 4.0, the color changed and the fluorescence quenched. In higher pHs, fluorescence decreased as well, this is due to the SiO2 layer can be ionized and surface defects is occurred at high pH [66]. Fig. 4D show the stability of the whole nanocomposite B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs. As one can see, it is stable for 10 weeks, while B-CuNCs@ZIF-8 was stabile for 2 months and pure Y-CDs have stability for 3 to 4 weeks. Thus, our nanocomposite led to enhance in the stability of the probe.

The fluorescence response of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP to detection of AA are shown in Fig. 5 A. The emission intensity of the yellow peak gradually decreases and the fluorescence emission of the blue peak remains constant with the increase of AA concentrations. The fluorescence intensity ratio of F420/F560 exhibits a linear fluorescence relationship for AA determination in the concentrations range from 4 to 20 µM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9980 (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was 1.56 µM (when signal/noise = 3). In addition, the fluorescence spectra of non-imprinted nanocomposite (B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs) was recorded in Fig. 5C. The fluorescence emission of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIPs does not quench dramatically comparing to B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs due to the high affinity of the template cavities made in the MIP. Therefore, the calibration curve of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@NIP show in Fig. 5D, and a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9838.

To investigate the fluorescence quenching, Stern–Volmer equation was plotted, (I0/I) = 1 + Ksv [Q]. Where, I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of fluorophore with and without quencher molecules, [Q] is the quencher concentration and KSV is the proportionality constant known as Stern–Volmer constant, which reflects the quenching efficiency or ease of access of the fluorophore to quencher. A greater KSV value indicates that the fluorophore is more sensitive to that specific analyte.[67] Fig. 6 Ashows the Stern–Volmer plot of Fo/F vs concentration of AA which exhibits linearity within the investigated concentrations. Fig. 6B shows the absorbance of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP (a) without AA and (b) B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP with AA. The target, AA, caused a blue shift due to a complexation or interaction assigning for the static quenching. The formation of a non-fluorescent fluorophore–quencher combination is the source of fluorescence quenching in static quenching.[68].

The application of the B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIPs probe for detection of AA in vitamin C tablets and body fluid serum was performed. The spike recoveries for quantitative determination of AA in Vitamin C tablets and serum carried out by adding different concentrations of AA (4.0 µM, 8.0 µM, and 12.0 µM). The results displayed in Table 1 and the results showed that the recoveries of AA were in the ranges 92.50 % to 106.11 % for the Vitamin C tablets and in the ranges 81.69 % to 93.89 % for the serum sample. The data indicated the applicability of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence MIP-MOF based sensor for the determination of AA in pharmaceutical formulation and the body fluid.
Table 2 shows a comparison for the reported methods in literature with our proposed probe. As shown in the Table, our nanocomposite has a reliable LOD and linearity range.

One of the limitations in our work is in application on the real samples, serum fluid for example, due to using 390 nm as an excitation light source. Some molecules can absorb such excitation light and filters off the light source and weaken the signal of the probe. For example, a collagen, NADH, fatty acid, Vitamin K, and vitamin D. Regardless of this, our probe showed very good accuracy and precision results, for both pharmaceutical formulation samples and human serum fluid.

In summary, cavities for AA were surface imprinted on encapsulated ZIF-8 with B-CuNCs and Y-CDs. Ratiomety-based biosensor was developed successfully, where blue emission of B-CuNCs served as reference, and Y-CDs were probe for AA. The emission of B-CuNCs was enhanced after encapsulation in the ZIF-8. The combination, B-CuNCs@ZIF-8/Y-CDs@MIP, produced sensitive (LOD as small as 1.5 μM), highly selective, and highly stable probe (even for two months). This ratiometry-based approach was successfully used for detection AA in a various drug sample and human serum fluid with satisfactory recoveries 92.50 % to 106.11 % and 81.69 % to 93.89 % respectively. In future work, one can design visual-based mode since there is a color tonality with variation of AA concentration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
